DM — Scene 3 — T2DM Algorithms: The Escalation Staircase & A1c Target Dial

🎯 Look at the lit symbol — say out loud what it is and what it represents (and the trap). Then reveal.

What you see
What it encodes
The board trap
Use ← → arrow keys · click a dot to jump · ▶ plays & narrates the scene
⚠️ Board traps 14
  1. Step 1 Lifestyle — WRONG: 'Start metformin alone and skip lifestyle counseling once a drug is on board.' Lifestyle modification is foundational and continues lifelong alongside every pharmacologic step. Discriminator: drugs are added TO lifestyle, never instead of it.
  2. Step 2 Metformin — WRONG: 'A patient with new T2DM and A1c 7.4% should start a sulfonylurea because it lowers glucose fastest.' Metformin is first-line for nearly everyone; sulfonylureas cause hypoglycemia and weight gain and are not preferred initial therapy. Discriminator: default monotherapy = metformin unless contraindicated.
  3. Step 3 Dual therapy — WRONG: 'A T2DM patient with HFrEF at A1c 6.8% on metformin needs no additional drug because they are at goal.' An SGLT2i is indicated for the heart-failure benefit independent of glycemic control. Discriminator: pick the second agent by ASCVD/HF/CKD comorbidity, not by whether A1c is already <7%.
  4. Step 4 Triple therapy — WRONG: 'Add a sulfonylurea as the third agent to a patient already on a GLP-1 RA without adjusting it.' Stacking insulin-secretagogues raises hypoglycemia risk; doses must be reduced and weight-neutral/cardioprotective agents are preferred. Discriminator: build triple therapy around complementary, low-hypoglycemia agents.
  5. Step 5 Injectable — WRONG: 'Any T2DM patient needing an injectable should start basal insulin first.' Non-catabolic patients should generally get a GLP-1 RA before insulin. Discriminator: insulin-first is reserved for catabolic presentations (see next), otherwise GLP-1 RA is preferred.
  6. Insulin if catabolic — WRONG: 'A newly diagnosed lean patient with A1c 11%, ketonuria, and weight loss should start metformin and recheck in 3 months.' Catabolic/symptomatic hyperglycemia requires INSULIN now (and consider T1DM/LADA workup). Discriminator: ketosis + weight loss + very high A1c = insulin first, not a slow oral titration.
  7. Forbidden combo: no GLP-1 + DPP-4 — WRONG: 'Add sitagliptin (DPP-4i) to a patient already on semaglutide (GLP-1 RA) for extra incretin effect.' These share the incretin pathway and should not be combined. Discriminator: GLP-1 RA + DPP-4i = redundant; pick one incretin-based agent.
  8. Preferred combos w/ SGLT2 & GLP-1 — WRONG: 'SGLT2i and GLP-1 RA cannot be used together because both are newer agents.' They act by distinct mechanisms (glycosuria vs incretin) and are commonly combined for additive cardiorenal benefit. Discriminator: the only forbidden incretin overlap is GLP-1 RA + DPP-4i, not SGLT2i + GLP-1 RA.
  9. A1c target 7% default dial — WRONG: 'Aim for A1c <7% in every diabetic regardless of age or frailty.' The <7% default must be loosened for frail/elderly or tightened for low-risk young patients. Discriminator: <7% is the DEFAULT, not a universal target.
  10. Tighter 6.5% (young/short DM) — WRONG: 'Push a frail 82-year-old on a sulfonylurea to A1c <6.5% for maximal protection.' Tight targets are for low-risk YOUNG patients; in the elderly this causes dangerous hypoglycemia with little benefit. Discriminator: <6.5% requires low hypoglycemia risk and long life expectancy.
  11. Looser 7.5-8.5% (frail/elderly) — WRONG: 'A nursing-home patient with dementia and hypoglycemia unawareness needs intensification to reach A1c 7%.' Such patients warrant a relaxed target (~8-8.5%) to prevent hypoglycemia. Discriminator: frailty/limited life expectancy/hypo unawareness -> loosen, do not intensify.
  12. CGM time-in-range — WRONG: 'A1c is always the most reliable marker, so CGM time-in-range is unnecessary.' A1c is misleading with anemia, hemoglobinopathies, CKD, recent transfusion, or pregnancy, where CGM/time-in-range is superior. Discriminator: use CGM metrics when A1c is unreliable or for granular hypoglycemia data.
  13. Pregnancy uses insulin — WRONG: 'Continue the patient's metformin/empagliflozin regimen unchanged when she becomes pregnant.' SGLT2i (and GLP-1 RA, statins) must be stopped; insulin is the standard. Discriminator: pregnancy = insulin-based therapy with tighter targets, not oral/incretin agents as first choice.
  14. Special-population stand — WRONG: 'Glyburide is a safe, cheap first-line agent for an 80-year-old with mild CKD.' Glyburide accumulates in renal impairment and causes severe, prolonged hypoglycemia in the elderly. Discriminator: in elderly/CKD prefer agents with low hypoglycemia risk (metformin, SGLT2i, GLP-1 RA, DPP-4i); avoid glyburide.
📖 Read-through — all 14 symbols
1. Step 1 Lifestyle
Step 1 for ALL patients: medical nutrition therapy, >=150 min/week moderate aerobic exercise, and 5-7% body-weight loss. Lifestyle continues at every subsequent step, not just at diagnosis. Even modest weight loss improves insulin sensitivity and can lower A1c by ~1-2%.
⚠️ WRONG: 'Start metformin alone and skip lifestyle counseling once a drug is on board.' Lifestyle modification is foundational and continues lifelong alongside every pharmacologic step. Discriminator: drugs are added TO lifestyle, never instead of it.
2. Step 2 Metformin
Step 2: metformin (start 500 mg daily with meals, titrate to 1000 mg BID) is first-line for essentially all T2DM unless contraindicated. Mechanism: decreases hepatic gluconeogenesis (AMPK), weight-neutral, no hypoglycemia. Reassess A1c at ~3 months. Hold if eGFR <30 (lactic-acidosis risk).
⚠️ WRONG: 'A patient with new T2DM and A1c 7.4% should start a sulfonylurea because it lowers glucose fastest.' Metformin is first-line for nearly everyone; sulfonylureas cause hypoglycemia and weight gain and are not preferred initial therapy. Discriminator: default monotherapy = metformin unless contraindicated.
3. Step 3 Dual therapy
Step 3: add a second agent chosen by COMORBIDITY, not by A1c alone. ASCVD/high CV risk -> GLP-1 RA (or SGLT2i with proven CV benefit). HFrEF/HFpEF or CKD -> SGLT2i. These benefits apply REGARDLESS of baseline A1c, even if A1c is already at goal on metformin.
⚠️ WRONG: 'A T2DM patient with HFrEF at A1c 6.8% on metformin needs no additional drug because they are at goal.' An SGLT2i is indicated for the heart-failure benefit independent of glycemic control. Discriminator: pick the second agent by ASCVD/HF/CKD comorbidity, not by whether A1c is already <7%.
4. Step 4 Triple therapy
Step 4: triple non-insulin combination (e.g., metformin + SGLT2i + GLP-1 RA) if still above target after dual therapy. Choose agents with complementary mechanisms and avoid overlapping/contraindicated pairs. Prioritize agents with cardiorenal benefit and low hypoglycemia risk.
⚠️ WRONG: 'Add a sulfonylurea as the third agent to a patient already on a GLP-1 RA without adjusting it.' Stacking insulin-secretagogues raises hypoglycemia risk; doses must be reduced and weight-neutral/cardioprotective agents are preferred. Discriminator: build triple therapy around complementary, low-hypoglycemia agents.
5. Step 5 Injectable
Step 5: when injectable therapy is needed, prefer a GLP-1 RA BEFORE basal insulin (greater A1c reduction, weight loss, CV benefit, less hypoglycemia) UNLESS the patient is catabolic. GLP-1 RAs and dual GIP/GLP-1 agonists (tirzepatide) are potent A1c-lowering injectables.
⚠️ WRONG: 'Any T2DM patient needing an injectable should start basal insulin first.' Non-catabolic patients should generally get a GLP-1 RA before insulin. Discriminator: insulin-first is reserved for catabolic presentations (see next), otherwise GLP-1 RA is preferred.
6. Insulin if catabolic
Start INSULIN promptly if catabolic features are present: unintentional weight loss, ketosis/ketonuria, symptoms of marked hyperglycemia, A1c >10% or glucose >=300, or to distinguish from possible T1DM/LADA. Basal insulin start: 0.1-0.2 u/kg/day (up to ~0.4) at bedtime, titrate by fasting glucose.
⚠️ WRONG: 'A newly diagnosed lean patient with A1c 11%, ketonuria, and weight loss should start metformin and recheck in 3 months.' Catabolic/symptomatic hyperglycemia requires INSULIN now (and consider T1DM/LADA workup). Discriminator: ketosis + weight loss + very high A1c = insulin first, not a slow oral titration.
7. Forbidden combo: no GLP-1 + DPP-4
Do NOT combine a GLP-1 RA with a DPP-4 inhibitor: both act on the incretin (GLP-1) axis, so DPP-4i adds no glycemic benefit on top of a GLP-1 RA and only adds cost/side effects. If starting a GLP-1 RA, stop the DPP-4i.
⚠️ WRONG: 'Add sitagliptin (DPP-4i) to a patient already on semaglutide (GLP-1 RA) for extra incretin effect.' These share the incretin pathway and should not be combined. Discriminator: GLP-1 RA + DPP-4i = redundant; pick one incretin-based agent.
8. Preferred combos w/ SGLT2 & GLP-1
Preferred comorbidity-driven pairings: metformin + SGLT2i (HF/CKD benefit), metformin + GLP-1 RA (ASCVD benefit, weight loss), and metformin + basal insulin when insulin is needed. SGLT2i + GLP-1 RA can be combined (different mechanisms) and gives additive cardiorenal protection.
⚠️ WRONG: 'SGLT2i and GLP-1 RA cannot be used together because both are newer agents.' They act by distinct mechanisms (glycosuria vs incretin) and are commonly combined for additive cardiorenal benefit. Discriminator: the only forbidden incretin overlap is GLP-1 RA + DPP-4i, not SGLT2i + GLP-1 RA.
9. A1c target 7% default dial
Default glycemic target is A1c <7% for most non-pregnant adults (ADA). This corresponds to an estimated average glucose ~154 mg/dL. The target is individualized up or down based on hypoglycemia risk, life expectancy, duration, and comorbidities.
⚠️ WRONG: 'Aim for A1c <7% in every diabetic regardless of age or frailty.' The <7% default must be loosened for frail/elderly or tightened for low-risk young patients. Discriminator: <7% is the DEFAULT, not a universal target.
10. Tighter 6.5% (young/short DM)
A tighter target (A1c <6.5%) is appropriate for younger patients, short DM duration, long life expectancy, no significant CVD, and low hypoglycemia risk — when it can be achieved without undue hypoglycemia or polypharmacy burden.
⚠️ WRONG: 'Push a frail 82-year-old on a sulfonylurea to A1c <6.5% for maximal protection.' Tight targets are for low-risk YOUNG patients; in the elderly this causes dangerous hypoglycemia with little benefit. Discriminator: <6.5% requires low hypoglycemia risk and long life expectancy.
11. Looser 7.5-8.5% (frail/elderly)
Looser targets: A1c 7.5-8% for advanced microvascular complications, extensive comorbidity, or longstanding hard-to-control DM; up to ~8-8.5% for frail elderly, hypoglycemia unawareness, or limited life expectancy. Avoiding hypoglycemia outweighs marginal glycemic gains here.
⚠️ WRONG: 'A nursing-home patient with dementia and hypoglycemia unawareness needs intensification to reach A1c 7%.' Such patients warrant a relaxed target (~8-8.5%) to prevent hypoglycemia. Discriminator: frailty/limited life expectancy/hypo unawareness -> loosen, do not intensify.
12. CGM time-in-range
Continuous glucose monitoring (CGM) metrics: time-in-range (70-180 mg/dL) >70%; time-below-range (<70) <4% and (<54) <1%; time-above-range (>180) <25%. Each ~10% increase in time-in-range correlates with roughly a 0.5-0.8% lower A1c. A Glucose Management Indicator (GMI) estimates A1c from CGM data.
⚠️ WRONG: 'A1c is always the most reliable marker, so CGM time-in-range is unnecessary.' A1c is misleading with anemia, hemoglobinopathies, CKD, recent transfusion, or pregnancy, where CGM/time-in-range is superior. Discriminator: use CGM metrics when A1c is unreliable or for granular hypoglycemia data.
13. Pregnancy uses insulin
In pregnancy, INSULIN is the preferred glucose-lowering therapy (does not cross the placenta, best safety data). Most non-insulin agents are avoided; GLP-1 RAs, SGLT2i, and statins are contraindicated. Glycemic goals are tighter (fasting <95, 1-h postprandial <140, 2-h <120 mg/dL).
⚠️ WRONG: 'Continue the patient's metformin/empagliflozin regimen unchanged when she becomes pregnant.' SGLT2i (and GLP-1 RA, statins) must be stopped; insulin is the standard. Discriminator: pregnancy = insulin-based therapy with tighter targets, not oral/incretin agents as first choice.
14. Special-population stand
Special populations: AVOID glyburide (long-acting sulfonylurea) in the elderly and in CKD (prolonged hypoglycemia) — it is a Beers-criteria drug. In severe obesity, favor GLP-1 RA or dual GIP/GLP-1 agonist (tirzepatide) for weight loss, and consider bariatric/metabolic surgery (esp. BMI >=35 with uncontrolled DM).
⚠️ WRONG: 'Glyburide is a safe, cheap first-line agent for an 80-year-old with mild CKD.' Glyburide accumulates in renal impairment and causes severe, prolonged hypoglycemia in the elderly. Discriminator: in elderly/CKD prefer agents with low hypoglycemia risk (metformin, SGLT2i, GLP-1 RA, DPP-4i); avoid glyburide.
